narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 61.9% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with stronger projected outcomes. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your position in the third-year class rankings, which stood out as the most influential factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure—your decile of 3 there was notably lower than the average of about 6 for those who passed. Coming in second was your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward success, as it exceeds the typical 3.24 seen among bar passers. Your LSAT score of 33 followed closely, contributing toward the failure prediction since it falls short of the 37.27 average for successful examinees. While your upper-middle family income aligns well with the majority of passers and your choice to study full-time matches the norm for them, these played smaller roles compared to your grades and test scores. Your first-year decile of 5 was middling relative to passers' average of 6, but it wasn't among the top drivers here. Overall, your profile compares unfavorably to admitted students mainly due to that third-year ranking and LSAT, despite strengths like your solid undergrad performance. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting patterns from past bar outcomes to guide admissions. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, as even a modest improvement could shift your predicted odds significantly. Strengthening your recent academic standing through targeted study habits or additional coursework might also help rebuild that third-year decile in future applications. Reach out to admissions advisors for personalized feedback, and explore prep programs tailored for the bar to demonstrate growth. With focused effort on these areas, you can reapply stronger next cycle.
